hook: A hand-painted pre-war tailor's advertisement still legible on a Praga tenement, signed by the man who painted it.
facts: On the façade of the c.1890 tenement at Brzeska 21 survives a painted inscription reading "KRAWIEC MĘSKI" (men's tailor).
It advertised the tailoring workshop of Moszek Szatz, noted on the property in 1930.
The advertisement was painted by an artist named Mordka Rubinsztajn, who signed the work — a rare survival of a signed interwar wall ad.
Brzeska is one of the best-preserved pre-war streets in Praga-Północ, which escaped the wartime destruction that levelled the left bank.
w: A hand-painted tailor's advertisement, still legible on a Praga tenement after a century -- and, rarest of all, signed by the man who painted it, Mordka Rubinsztajn. It hawked the workshop of Moszek Szatz around 1930. Brzeska is one of the best-preserved pre-war streets in Praga, spared the ruin that flattened the left bank.

hook: A mural on a parish wall recreates the lost tenement immortalised in the most famous song of the Warsaw Uprising.
facts: The mural at Żytnia 1, on the wall of the parish house of the Sanctuary of St Faustina, was unveiled on 2 August 2023.
It depicts "Pałacyk Michla" (the Michler tenement), defended by Sgt Janusz Brochwicz-Lewiński "Gryf" and his platoon at the start of the Warsaw Uprising.
The building inspired the insurgent song "Pałacyk Michla", written by Józef Szczepański "Ziutek".
The mural was designed by Jacek Widor, an illustrator and comics author.
w: On a parish wall in Wola, a 2023 mural rebuilds a tenement that no longer stands -- the Palacyk Michla, held by 'Gryf' and his platoon in the first days of the 1944 Uprising. The fight gave Poland one of its most-sung insurgent songs, written by 'Ziutek'. Jacek Widor drew it. A vanished house, kept alive in paint and in chorus.

hook: A whimsical architectural mural honours a Polish president who quietly taught economics at the school it stands beside.
facts: The mural covers a wall in front of Building M of SGH Warsaw School of Economics at Madalińskiego 6/8.
It was unveiled on 22 December 2022 and depicts Stanisław Wojciechowski, the second president of the Second Polish Republic.
Wojciechowski lectured at the school (then the Higher School of Commerce) from 1919 to 1939, including during his 1922–1926 presidency.
It was the first Warsaw mural by Tytus Brzozowski, the architect-painter known for his fantastical watercolour cityscapes, executed in Mokotów.
w: A whimsical architectural mural beside the School of Economics honours a man who taught economics here for twenty years -- and happened to be President of Poland while doing it. Stanislaw Wojciechowski lectured from 1919 to 1939, right through his term in office. Tytus Brzozowski painted it in 2022.

hook: A 1970 mermaid-on-a-book neon glows nightly over Grójecka, kept alive by neighbours after the library that owned it moved away.
facts: The neon — a Syrenka (mermaid) seated on an open book, the emblem of Warsaw's public libraries — was made in 1970 by the LUMEN neon cooperative.
It hangs in situ on the former children's library at Grójecka 81/87 in Ochota.
When the library relocated in 2011, the "BIBLIOTEKA PUBLICZNA" lettering went to the Neon Museum but the mermaid stayed on the wall.
After it was vandalised in March 2011, an Ochota community fundraiser ("Włącz neon") restored it for the Night of Museums; it is lit each evening under the Neon Museum's care.
w: A 1970 neon mermaid perched on an open book -- the old emblem of Warsaw's libraries -- still glows each night over Grojecka. When the children's library it belonged to moved out in 2011, the lettering went to the Neon Museum but the mermaid stayed. Vandalised that year, the Ochota neighbours fundraised to relight her.

hook: A wall of floating hats and a stanza about journeys never taken pay tribute to the poet who gave this new estate its name.
facts: The mural is on a residential block of the Żoliborz Artystyczny estate at Powązkowska 7/13, whose quarter is named for poet Jerzy Ficowski.
Designed by Piotr Młodożeniec, it was painted in June 2014.
Its hats reference Ficowski's poem "Moje niedoszłe podróże" (My Unrealised Journeys), with a verse painted on an adjacent wall.
Ficowski (1924–2006) was a poet, songwriter and the great chronicler of Polish Roma and of Bruno Schulz.
w: A wall of floating hats and a painted stanza honour the poet who gave this new Zoliborz estate its name. Piotr Mlodozeniec drew it in 2014, the hats from his poem 'My Unrealised Journeys', a verse on the next wall. Ficowski chronicled Poland's Roma and championed Bruno Schulz. Journeys never taken, fixed to a tower block.

hook: Wall paintings of zodiac signs and Jerusalem, hidden for decades in a Praga courtyard, are the only surviving polychromes of a Warsaw prayer house.
facts: In a courtyard annexe of the tenements at Targowa 50/52 (now the Museum of Warsaw's Praga) survive painted murals from a pre-war Jewish prayer house.
They show an incomplete zodiac cycle, Jews praying at the Western Wall, and Rachel's Tomb in Bethlehem; the surviving layer dates to a 1934 repainting.
Historians Janusz Sujecki and Jarosław Zieliński accidentally rediscovered the paintings in March 1996.
They are regarded as the only authentic surviving polychromes of a Jewish prayer house in the Mazovia region.
w: Hidden for decades in a Praga courtyard: painted zodiac signs, Jews at the Western Wall, Rachel's Tomb -- the only surviving wall paintings of a Warsaw Jewish prayer house. Two historians stumbled on them in 1996; the surviving layer dates to a 1934 repainting. The tenement holds the Museum of Warsaw's Praga branch now.

hook: A Wola gable-end portrait of Czesław Niemen, unveiled to the strains of "Sen o Warszawie".
facts: The mural is painted on a building at Młynarska 33 in Wola.
It was unveiled on 4 October 2021 and designed by Tomasz Majewski.
At the unveiling, Niemen's song "Sen o Warszawie" (lyrics by Marek Gaszyński) was played from loudspeakers.
The project was initiated and funded by the Stowarzyszenie Przyjaciół Warszawy (Friends of Warsaw Association).
w: A gable-end portrait of Czeslaw Niemen, the great voice of Polish rock, gazing out over Wola. Tomasz Majewski painted it in 2021; at the unveiling, loudspeakers played 'Sen o Warszawie' -- Niemen's dream of Warsaw -- to the crowd below. The Friends of Warsaw paid for it. A singer's face, looking back at the city he sang.

hook: A sculpted basilisk crowns the doorway of the Old Town's most legend-haunted tenement, where a monster's stony gaze once guarded cellar treasure.
facts: Stands at Rynek Starego Miasta 5 on the Dekert (north) side of the Old Town Market Square; the tenement's origins go back to 1470 (built by Jan Kirstian).
Burned in 1607 and rebuilt in 1614 by mayor Stanisław Jeleń; destroyed in 1944 and faithfully reconstructed in 1952-1953 (architects Mieczysław Kuzma and Lesław Nowakowski).
The basilisk sculpture above the entrance was made by Jerzy Brabander during the 1952-53 reconstruction.
Since the post-war rebuild the building has housed Restauracja Bazyliszek, one of the best-known Old Town restaurants.
legend: Legend says a basilisk guarded treasure in the cellars near Krzywe Koło street, turning to stone anyone who met its gaze; a shoemaker's apprentice (in some versions a condemned man) defeated it by holding up a mirror, so the creature was killed by the reflection of its own eyes. The tale was popularised by Artur Oppman's 'Legendy warszawskie'.
w: A stone basilisk crowns the doorway of this Old Town tenement, marking the city's most-told legend. The story goes a monster lurked in the cellars near Krzywe Kolo, turning to stone any who met its eyes -- until an apprentice held up a mirror and killed it with its own reflection. The house burned in 1944 and rose again stone for stone.

hook: A wedge-shaped house barely two metres wide squeezes into the corner of the tiny Kanonia square — a relic of the old tax dodge of building narrow at the street.
facts: Sits at the corner of the triangular plac Kanonia, tucked behind St John's Archcathedral in the Old Town.
Its street facade is only about 2 metres wide (roughly the span of outstretched arms); the house is wedge-shaped and widens considerably toward the Vistula escarpment, where its true size is visible.
Built in the 18th century, much later than the surrounding canons' houses, as infill development.
The narrow frontage reflected a historic tax calculated on a building's width at street level — so owners built thin facades to pay less. Often described as one of the narrowest houses in Europe.
w: A wedge of a house barely two metres wide -- an arm-span -- squeezes into the tiny Kanonia square behind the cathedral. It was built thin on purpose: the old tax fell on a building's width at the street, so owners kept frontages narrow and let the house fatten out of sight toward the river. Often called one of Europe's narrowest.

hook: A cracked 17th-century bronze bell stands on the cobbles of Kanonia square — touch it, walk around it once, and Warsaw folklore says your wish comes true.
facts: The bell was cast in 1646 in Warsaw by royal bell-founder Daniel Tym for the Corpus Christi collegiate church in Jarosław (commissioned by Jan Mikołaj Daniłowicz).

It never reached Jarosław — most likely because of a casting flaw that left it cracked and unringable — and after WWI it was rediscovered at a scrapyard in Podolia and acquired by the National Museum.
It bears a Latin inscription from Psalm 150 and reliefs of Christ, the Virgin Mary and John the Baptist, with a cartouche of four coats of arms.
It stands free on the small triangular plac Kanonia, just behind St John's Archcathedral.
legend: A popular Warsaw superstition holds that to make a wish come true you should close your eyes, think of your wish, touch the bell and walk around it once; some versions say touching it brings wealth.
w: A cracked bronze bell stands free on the cobbles of Kanonia square -- cast in 1646 for a church in Jaroslaw it never reached, most likely flawed and unringable from the start. Found at a scrapyard after the Great War. Warsaw superstition says to close your eyes, touch it and walk around it once, and your wish comes true.

hook: A bullet-pocked brewery wall at the corner of Waliców and Pereca is one of only three surviving stretches of the Warsaw Ghetto boundary — now wedged into a glass apartment block.
facts: The wall is a surviving fragment of Hermann Jung's brewery, founded in 1854 at the corner of Waliców and Ceglana (today Pereca) streets.
It formed the boundary of the Warsaw Ghetto from November 1940 to August 1942.
It is one of the three best-known surviving ghetto-wall fragments — the other two being the courtyards at Sienna 55 and Złota 62.

The brick remnant is densely scarred from wartime fighting; incorporated into the Aurum office building in 1999-2000, then re-integrated into the ground floor of a new residential building after Aurum was demolished (the historic gateway disappeared during that 2017-19 rebuild).
w: A bullet-pocked brewery wall at the corner of Walicow and Pereca -- one of only three surviving stretches of the Warsaw Ghetto boundary. It belonged to Hermann Jung's 1854 brewery and marked the ghetto's edge from 1940 to 1942. Densely scarred by the fighting, it now stands wedged into the ground floor of a glass apartment block.

hook: A lone Art Nouveau tenement stranded between two postwar blocks on Marszałkowska — the last survivor of the street's prewar frontage, fronted by a wall that was never meant to be seen.
facts: Built in 1905-1906 for Izaak Rothberg and Zygmunt Frumkin to a design by architect Ludwik Panczakiewicz — one of the few Warsaw tenements built in full Art Nouveau (secesja) style.
It is the only surviving relic of the prewar buildings that once formed the western frontage of the northern stretch of Marszałkowska.
The Uprising-era fire gutted the front building; its burned remains were demolished, leaving a former internal courtyard elevation — never intended as a facade — now facing Marszałkowska Street.
It survives squeezed between two large postwar modernist blocks just south of plac Zbawiciela; the city has restored its Art Nouveau stucco and historic entrance doors.
w: A lone Art Nouveau tenement stranded between two postwar blocks on Marszalkowska -- the last survivor of the street's prewar frontage. The Uprising gutted its front building; the burned shell came down, leaving an inner courtyard wall, never meant to be seen, now facing the street. Built in 1905, its secesja stucco restored.

hook: A seven-tonne granite obelisk on the corner of Rondo Dmowskiego is Warsaw's official kilometre zero — the point every road sign distance in Poland is measured from.
facts: Stands at the north-western corner of Rondo Dmowskiego, where Aleje Jerozolimskie meets Marszałkowska.
A roughly 1.5 m square, ~2.5 m tall block of polished granite weighing about seven tonnes.
It carries plaques listing distances to European capitals and to Poland's then-voivodeship capitals (the latter per the 1950s administrative division).
Erected between 1952 and 1955 during the building of Plac Defilad and the reshaping of streets around the Palace of Culture; it marks the zero point from which road-sign distances are counted.
w: A seven-tonne granite obelisk on the corner of Rondo Dmowskiego marks Warsaw's kilometre zero -- the point every road-sign distance in Poland is measured from. Plaques on it count the miles to European capitals and to the old voivodeship towns. It went up in the early 1950s as the Palace of Culture rose nearby.

hook: The theatre Stefan Jaracz founded inside a railwaymen's union hall, later a stage for Cybulski, Holoubek and Janda.
facts: Founded 1928 by the actor Stefan Jaracz (with Zygmunt Chmielewski and Janusz Dziewoński); the theatre is named after Jaracz, who is its patron.
It occupies the building of the Railway Workers' Trade Union; the company first played at ul. Czerwonego Krzyża 20 and now sits at ul. Stefana Jaracza 2.
Under German occupation, from October 1943 the Nazi 'Kleines Theater der Stadt Warschau' operated in the building.
Actors associated with the theatre include Zbigniew Cybulski, Gustaw Holoubek and Krystyna Janda.
In October 2020 it opened a fourth stage, 'Scena 20', at Wybrzeże Kościuszkowskie 35; its artistic director since 2021 is Artur Tyszkiewicz.
w: The theatre the great actor Stefan Jaracz founded in 1928, inside a railwaymen's union hall on Powisle. Under occupation the Nazis ran their own 'Kleines Theater' in the building; after the war, a stage for Cybulski, Holoubek and Janda. It still plays under Jaracz's name. A workers' hall that turned into one of Warsaw's great theatres.

hook: A Socrealist comedy theatre on Żoliborz, raised in a building by the modernist Brukalskis, that opened with Cole Porter.
facts: Located at ul. Juliusza Słowackiego 19a in Żoliborz, about 650 m from the Plac Wilsona metro station.
The building (1948-1954) was designed by the celebrated architect couple Stanisław and Barbara Brukalski.
The theatre opened in 1954 (initially as 'Teatr na Żoliborzu') and took the name Teatr Komedia in 1957.
Its first production was Cole Porter's musical 'Kiss Me, Kate', with Polish text by Janusz Minkiewicz and Antoni Marianowicz.
It was renamed Teatr Północny from 1990, reverting to Teatr Komedia in 1994; the main auditorium seats 483.
w: A comedy theatre on Zoliborz, housed in a 1950s building by the modernist Brukalskis, that opened in 1954 with Cole Porter's 'Kiss Me, Kate'. It began as the Teatr na Zoliborzu, took the name Komedia in 1957, briefly went 'Polnocny', then changed back. Its main house seats 483. Cole Porter under a socrealist roof.

hook: Poland's great film factory on Chełmska, home of the Polish Film Chronicle and the studio where Wajda shot.
facts: Founded in 1949 as Wytwórnia Filmów Dokumentalnych, on the grounds of a former palace estate at ul. Chełmska 21 (the site borders today's Park Sielecki).
It produced the Polska Kronika Filmowa (Polish Film Chronicle) and dozens of documentaries a year.
From 1961 it began making feature films; two new sound stages opened in 1960 and output reached around 20 features a year in the 1970s.
Andrzej Wajda was among the directors who realised films at the studio.
In 2013 it was reorganised by the Minister of Culture into a state cultural institution; it still operates as WFDiF.
w: Poland's great film factory on Chelmska, founded in 1949 on an old palace estate. From here came the Polska Kronika Filmowa, the newsreel that opened screenings for decades, and dozens of documentaries a year. Feature films followed from 1961; Wajda shot here. Still working, eight decades on. Where the country watched itself.

hook: A surviving 1960s milk bar built into Halina Skibniewska's garden estate, still serving pierogi under its modernist V-roof.
facts: Located at ul. Zygmunta Krasińskiego 36, on the corner of Broniewskiego, in the Sady Żoliborskie housing estate.
The pavilion was designed by architect Halina Skibniewska in the 1960s as the service block of the Sady Żoliborskie estate, with a characteristic modernist V-shaped roof.
It is run by the WSS Społem cooperative and is counted among the last traditional 'bar mleczny' (milk bars) preserving their PRL-era character and menu.
It keeps milk-bar hours: roughly 7:00-19:00 on weekdays and 9:00-17:00 at weekends.
w: A surviving 1960s milk bar built into Halina Skibniewska's leafy Sady Zoliborskie estate, still ladling pierogi under its modernist V-roof. Skibniewska designed the pavilion as the estate's service block; it keeps PRL milk-bar hours and prices, run by the old Spolem cooperative. One of the last bary mleczne with its character intact.

hook: The café named for the Mickiewicz monument nearby, immortalised in Tyrmand's 'Zły' and used by Kieślowski for the Dekalog.
facts: Located in the Kestner tenement at Krakowskie Przedmieście 27, on the corner of ul. Kozia; a café reportedly stood here as early as 1827.
The name nods to the nearby Adam Mickiewicz monument (Telimena being a character from his 'Pan Tadeusz').
Destroyed in 1944, the building and café were rebuilt by the late 1940s and became a haunt of University of Warsaw students and staff.
Leopold Tyrmand featured Telimena in his cult 1955 novel 'Zły' ('The Man with the White Eyes').
A scene from Krzysztof Kieślowski's 'Dekalog' / 'A Short Film About Love' (Decalogue Six) was filmed at the café.
w: A cafe has stood at this Krakowskie Przedmiescie corner since 1827, named for a character in 'Pan Tadeusz' after the Mickiewicz monument nearby. Rebuilt after 1944, it drew the university crowd and slipped into Tyrmand's novel 'Zly' -- and Kieslowski filmed a scene of his 'Dekalog' here. A literary address, poured one coffee at a time.

hook: The last Warsaw address of poet Julian Tuwim, marked by a sandstone plaque on a rebuilt 1822 tenement.
facts: Julian Tuwim lived at Nowy Świat 25 from 1951 until his death in 1953; a commemorative plaque is set on the front facade.
The plaque is carved in sandstone and was unveiled in the second half of the 1950s.
Tuwim died on 27 December 1953 in Zakopane, the year his residence at this address ended.
The classicist tenement at Nowy Świat 25 dates from 1822 and was rebuilt in the early 1950s after wartime destruction.
w: The last Warsaw home of Julian Tuwim, the dazzling, much-loved poet of the Skamander circle, marked by a sandstone plaque on a rebuilt 1822 tenement. He lived here from 1951 until his death two years later, in 1953. The classicist house behind the plaque was raised from wartime ruins. A quiet address for a loud, glittering voice.

hook: The grey 1970s prefab estate Kieślowski chose for the Dekalog — the towers nicknamed 'Manhattan' where Tomek spies through a telescope.
facts: Kieślowski set the residential scenes of his 'Dekalog' on the WSM estate between ulica Inflancka, Dzika and Stawki, north of the former ghetto.
The 1970s blocks were built using prefabricated reinforced-concrete 'H-frame' (rama H) technology.
The same estate served the spun-off features 'Krótki film o miłości' (A Short Film About Love) and 'Krótki film o zabijaniu' (A Short Film About Killing); locals nicknamed the towers 'Manhattan'.
Kieślowski had considered estates at Stegny, Służew, Marymont and Ursynów before choosing the Stawki/Inflancka/Dzika location as the most interesting.
w: The grey 1970s prefab estate Kieslowski chose for his 'Dekalog' -- the H-frame concrete towers north of the old ghetto that locals nicknamed 'Manhattan'. He shot the cycle's residential scenes here, and the two spun-off features, including the boy with the telescope in 'A Short Film About Love'. He scouted four estates before this one.
